caveats: a: No six-digit occupational or task dataset was located.
a: Coatings plants and high-speed shingle lines have different labor mixes, making an industry aggregate imprecise.
rho: Process digitization indicates feasibility but does not measure AI implementation success.
rho: Warranty and product-liability risk can keep human approval in place even when models perform well.
e: The frozen firm count is an ESTIMATE and the paper/forest-products margin bridge may not match roofing-product economics.
e: Repeat distributor sales remain product revenue and do not automatically satisfy the outsourced-service lens.
s5: Gallup is neither industry- nor size-specific and measures intentions rather than consummated transfers.
s5: A plant acquisition by a strategic manufacturer may be an asset purchase rather than a qualifying firm control transfer.
q: No representative contracts or pass-through study were located.
q: Brand strength and channel concentration can move retention far outside the industry range.
d5: No current public six-digit shipment forecast or reroofing-volume series was located.
d5: Storm incidence, insurance practices, housing turnover, interest rates, and material substitution can materially shift demand.
o: An accountable manufacturer can remain necessary while production consolidates into large firms outside the lens.
o: Operator requirement does not imply current labor intensity or preserve independent contract-manufacturer demand.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Primarily per-unit, bundle, roll, gallon, or truckload product sales through distributors and contractor channels; qualifying outsourced-service revenue is limited to private-label or contract manufacturing with repeat external customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Roofing installation contractors, building-material distributors, captive internal manufacturing units, integrated plants outside the EBITDA band, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market manufacturers of asphalt shingles, roll roofing, saturated felts, roof and waterproofing coatings, and related asphalt materials that serve recurring external distributors, contractors, private-label customers, or genuine contract-manufacturing accounts.

a.bridge: EPA process descriptions and BLS broader NAICS 324 occupations proxy the task mix rather than measuring wage-weighted, current unautomated tasks in NAICS 324122 LMM firms. The range assigns exposure to production planning, visual inspection support, recipe and process analytics, predictive maintenance, procurement, forecasting, customer service, documentation, and compliance reporting, while treating material handling, line operation, maintenance, laboratory sampling, packaging, and field verification as low-substitution; broader petroleum-sector occupations may understate commercial and packaging work in roofing plants.
caveats: No six-digit occupational or task dataset was located.
Coatings plants and high-speed shingle lines have different labor mixes, making an industry aggregate imprecise.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task and current-automation audit from at least ten independent shingle, roll-roofing, and coatings plants, split across line operation, laboratory, maintenance, quality, warehousing, planning, sales, customer service, regulatory work, and management.
a.rationale: Continuous shingle and roll lines generate structured process and quality data that AI can use for anomaly detection, maintenance, scheduling, inspection support, and yield improvement. EPA describes a physical sequence of asphalt preparation, coating, mineral surfacing, cooling, cutting or laminating, stacking, and packaging, so most direct production work remains tied to equipment and materials.

d5.bridge: Census housing starts measure new residential units rather than reroofing, commercial roofing, coatings, product mix, or output of eligible LMM contract manufacturers. New construction is only one demand component and may understate replacement-driven stability, while housing units do not directly translate to asphalt roof area because multifamily structure, regional material choice, and roof size differ.
caveats: No current public six-digit shipment forecast or reroofing-volume series was located.
Storm incidence, insurance practices, housing turnover, interest rates, and material substitution can materially shift demand.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year forecasts and historical shipments split among reroofing, new residential, commercial, roll roofing, and coatings, combined with roof-age cohorts, insurance claims, regional material shares, housing completions, and eligible manufacturers' order books.
d5.rationale: Roof replacement from aging and weather damage supplies recurring demand, while new housing and commercial construction add cyclicality. Census reported May 2026 housing starts at a 1.177 million annual rate, down 8.7% from May 2025, illustrating short-run weakness. Over five years, the base assumes approximately stable constant-quality quantity as repair demand offsets construction volatility and competition from metal, tile, membranes, and longer-lived materials.

caveats: The frozen firm count is an ESTIMATE and the paper/forest-products margin bridge may not match roofing-product economics.
Repeat distributor sales remain product revenue and do not automatically satisfy the outsourced-service lens.
e.change_evidence: A legal-entity and contract review of the 49 estimated firms identifying contract-manufacturing or private-label service revenue, external-customer recurrence, brand ownership, affiliate sales, normalized EBITDA, plant and permit ownership, warranty obligations, and environmental liabilities.
e.rationale: Most firms in the code manufacture branded building products rather than provide a recurring outsourced service. A limited subset of private-label, toll, or contract manufacturers may fit the lens. Eligibility is further reduced by scale, vertical integration, product and warranty liabilities, environmental permits, customer concentration, asset condition, and the possibility that the provided margin bridge misclassifies capital-intensive plants into the LMM band.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 324122 ($1,451,468,199) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 324122 ($15,241,200,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1465 (margins.json[32]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Paper/Forest Products EBITDA/Sales 14.65% (mid-range for wood/paper/plastics)); revenue/employee $1,376,181 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 324122); class <5: 22 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,053,045 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $300,771 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 15 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,946,667 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $871,187 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 12 firms, avg revenue/firm $17,151,167 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,512,646 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 30 firms, avg revenue/firm $29,377,600 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,303,818 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 7 firms, avg revenue/firm $64,217,714 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $9,407,895 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 18 firms, avg revenue/firm $753,898,167 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $110,446,081 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 49

caveats: An accountable manufacturer can remain necessary while production consolidates into large firms outside the lens.
Operator requirement does not imply current labor intensity or preserve independent contract-manufacturer demand.
o.change_evidence: Regulatory and operating evidence from highly automated roofing-material plants showing remote-operation scope, minimum staffing, unattended hours, autonomous quality release, maintenance requirements, product-liability responsibility, and customer self-service share.
o.rationale: Any remaining physical shingles, rolls, or coatings require an accountable operator to manage hot asphalt, substrates, mineral surfacing or formulations, emissions controls, product specifications, continuous lines, packaging, release testing, warranties, and delivery. AI can reduce staffing and improve decisions, but software-only self-service cannot manufacture, certify, and stand behind physical roofing products.

caveats: No representative contracts or pass-through study were located.
Brand strength and channel concentration can move retention far outside the industry range.
q.change_evidence: SKU- and customer-level price-cost-margin bridges before and after automation, including asphalt and granule costs, rebates, freight, promotions, contract resets, private-label bids, warranty claims, volumes, and competitor price moves over three years.
q.rationale: Manufacturers may retain gains through branded pricing, distributor relationships, reliable supply, color and performance differentiation, and warranties. However, asphalt inputs, freight, channel rebates, retailer bargaining, private-label tenders, competitor pricing, and periodic contract renewal expose productivity gains to customers. Pure contract manufacturing should retain less than differentiated branded products.

caveats: Process digitization indicates feasibility but does not measure AI implementation success.
Warranty and product-liability risk can keep human approval in place even when models perform well.
rho.change_evidence: Two-year before-and-after deployment evidence for machine-vision inspection, predictive maintenance, recipe optimization, production scheduling, demand forecasting, and customer-service automation, reporting labor hours, scrap, claims, downtime, changeover, and operator overrides.
rho.rationale: Planning, forecasting, procurement, document, and customer-service use cases are readily implementable, and process data support inspection and maintenance assistance. Realization is constrained by legacy control systems, sensor and image-label quality, formulation confidentiality, line-validation requirements, environmental permits, cybersecurity, changeover complexity, warranty exposure, and the cost of widespread defective product.

s5.bridge: Gallup found 22% of US employer-business owners across industries planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years. The range discounts stated plans for a tiny eligible population, strategic-buyer concentration, environmental and warranty diligence, financing needs, family/internal transfers, failed sales, and closures; mature owner demographics could make the general proxy conservative, while branded-product consolidation could move plants without leaving eligible standalone operations.
caveats: Gallup is neither industry- nor size-specific and measures intentions rather than consummated transfers.
A plant acquisition by a strategic manufacturer may be an asset purchase rather than a qualifying firm control transfer.
s5.change_evidence: Ten years of closed acquisitions, ownership changes, plant sales, closures, and permit transfers for NAICS 324122 from industry filings, state air agencies, PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, SBA lenders, and county property records, matched to lens eligibility and owner age.
s5.rationale: Mature manufacturing businesses can transfer, but plants require buyers able to assume permits, inventory, formulas, distribution relationships, warranty exposure, working capital, and capital expenditure. The eligible private-label or contract-manufacturing subset is likely much smaller than the frozen count, so completed qualifying transfers should be infrequent.

principal_driver: Continuous, data-rich production lines create repeatable opportunities in inspection, maintenance, scheduling, yield, and service administration.
principal_weakness: Nearly all industry revenue is product manufacturing rather than recurring outsourced service, leaving few plausible LMM eligible firms.

run_meta.title: Asphalt Shingle and Coating Materials Manufacturing

sources.supports: Describes asphalt roofing products and six main operations: felt saturation, coating, mineral surfacing, cooling and drying, finishing including cutting and laminating, and packaging, plus asphalt, filler, and granule support operations.
sources.title: AP-42 Chapter 11.2: Asphalt Roofing

sources.supports: Provides broader-subsector occupational context, including 5,230 mixing and blending machine setters, operators, and tenders and 4,980 first-line production supervisors in 2025.
sources.title: Petroleum and Coal Products Manufacturing: NAICS 324

sources.supports: States that asphalt processing and roofing manufacture significantly contribute to air pollution and requires new, modified, and reconstructed facilities to use demonstrated continuous-emission-reduction systems.
sources.title: Asphalt Processing and Asphalt Roofing Manufacture: New Source Performance Standards

sources.supports: Fall 2024 US survey found 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, while 5% planned closure and 5% had no plan.
sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: Reports May 2026 privately owned housing starts at a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 1.177 million, 8.7% below May 2025, and building permits at 1.413 million.
sources.title: Monthly New Residential Construction, May 2026

title: Asphalt Shingle and Coating Materials Manufacturing
